title: Packing Hacks to Save on Baggage Fees
excerpt: Carry-on strategy, family packing logic, airline-by-airline fee comparisons, and the 10-item system that eliminates baggage charges entirely for summer 2026 trips.

placeholderSections.heading: How Airline Baggage Fees Changed After Spirit's Collapse
placeholderSections.body: Spirit Airlines ceased operations on May 2, 2026, eliminating the industry's most extreme a-la-carte pricing model. Spirit charged $38–$55 for carry-on bags and $42–$65 for checked luggage on a roundtrip, and its departure has reshuffled the fee scenery. Frontier, Spirit's closest competitor, raised its carry-on fees to $45–$69 (up from $35–$55) within two weeks of Spirit's closure and increased checked bag fees by 15%. Allegiant similarly adjusted upward, with carry-on fees now hitting $50–$75. The low-cost vacuum has also emboldened legacy carriers: Delta raised its standard checked bag fee from $35 to $40 for advance purchase (still $50 at the gate) in early May. United and American held steady at $35/$45 advance/gate for first checked bags but eliminated the $5 discount for paying at booking vs. before departure. The net effect for summer 2026 travelers: a checked bag roundtrip now costs $70–$100 on legacy carriers and $84–$130 on ultra-low-cost airlines. Southwest,  still includes two free checked bags per passenger — making it the most luggage-friendly carrier in the country by a wide margin.

placeholderSections.heading: Carry-On Strategy: The Right Bag Makes the Difference
placeholderSections.body: Winning at carry-on starts with the bag itself. The personal item (a backpack, duffel, or tote measuring under 18x14x8 inches on most airlines) is free on every U.S. airline — including Frontier, Allegiant, and Spirit's successor carriers. The most effective personal item bags for summer travel: a 30–40L backpack that fits under the seat when not overstuffed (Osprey Daylite, Cotopaxi Batac 28L, or a cheap Amazon Basics 30L at $25). Pair this with a full-size carry-on spinner (22x14x9 inches for most airlines, 21.5x15.5x7.8 inches for cheaper carriers — check your airline's spec). A soft-sided duffel compresses easier into sizers than hard-shell cases. The Summer 2026 smart strategy: book airlines that include carry-on in their base fare or include it as a no-fee companion benefit. Delta, United, and American include carry-on in all fares — no extra charge. Frontier and Allegiant charge $45–$75 for overhead-bin carry-on but $0 for personal items. The play: pack everything into a well-structured personal item bag, wear your bulkiest items (jacket, heaviest shoes) on the flight, and use a collapsible duffel in your backpack for bringing purchases back.

placeholderSections.heading: Family Packing: Share Weight, Share Space, Share Costs
placeholderSections.body: A family of four can easily spend $200–$500 roundtrip on baggage alone — but smart family packing kills this cost. Rule one: one checked bag for the whole family is cheaper than individual bags. A single 50-lb checked bag costs $35–$40 on Delta/United/American. Even better: pack everyone's clothing into one large roller bag (check it) and have each family member carry a personal item backpack with their own essentials (electronics, medications, change of clothes). For a family of four on a 7-day summer trip, that's one checked bag ($35) plus four free personal items = $35 total bag fees. Compare to checking four bags at $35–$40 each: that's $140–$160 saved. Rule two: share toiletries. One family-sized shampoo, sunscreen, and toothpaste in the checked bag replaces four individual sets. Travel-size bottles for personal items only. Rule three: use vacuum compression bags (or the roll-and-pack method for cotton clothes) to fit two people's clothing into one suitcase. Summer clothing is light and compressible, so a family of four's worth of t-shirts, shorts, and swimwear fits in one large checked bag with room to spare.

placeholderSections.heading: Overweight and Oversized Fee Traps to Avoid
placeholderSections.body: Overweight bag fees are where airlines make their real money. Standard checked bag weight limits: most U.S. airlines allow 50 lbs max at the standard fee. Go over 50 lbs, and you're hit with a $70–$100 overweight fee (Delta charges $100 for 51–70 lbs; United charges $100 for 51–70 lbs; American charges $70 for 51–70 lbs). For bags 71–100 lbs, the fee jumps to $150–$200, and some airlines outright refuse bags over 50 lbs. The trap: families pack one big bag to save on per-bag fees, then overload past 50 lbs and pay more in overweight fees than they saved. Solution: weigh your bag at home before leaving. A cheap luggage scale ($10 on Amazon) pays for itself on the first trip. Better strategy: pack two lighter checked bags instead of one heavy one. On Southwest (two free bags), this costs nothing. On United (first bag $35 for advance), two checked bags = $70 vs. one 60-lb bag = $35 + $100 overweight = $135. Oversized baggage (over 62 linear inches or 158 cm) triggers a $75–$150 special handling fee. Call the airline 48 hours ahead to confirm oversized policy — don't find out at the check-in counter.

placeholderSections.heading: Airline-by-Airline Baggage Fee Comparison (Summer 2026)
placeholderSections.body: Here's the current fee scenery. Southwest: 2 free checked bags (50 lbs each), no change fees, 1 free carry-on + 1 free personal item. Best option for families by a wide margin. Delta: 1st bag $40 advance / $50 gate; 2nd bag $45 advance / $55 gate; overweight (51–70 lbs) $100; carry-on free. United: 1st bag $35 advance / $45 gate; 2nd bag $45 advance / $55 gate; overweight (51–70 lbs) $100; carry-on free. American: 1st bag $35 advance / $45 gate; 2nd bag $45 advance / $55 gate; overweight (51–70 lbs) $70; carry-on free. Frontier and Allegiant: 1st checked bag $42–$65 advance; 2nd checked bag $50–$70; carry-on (overhead bin) $45–$75; personal item $0. Breeze Airways: 1st checked bag $40; carry-on included in Nicer and Nicest fares but costs $30 in Nice (base) fare. JetBlue: Blue fare includes 1 carry-on; 1st checked bag $35; Blue Basic fare: 1 personal item only. Alaska: 1st checked bag $35; 2nd $45; carry-on free. Key insight: booking 21+ days in advance saves $5–$15 on most airlines. Paying at booking vs. at the airport saves $10–$20 per bag — always add bags during checkout.

placeholderSections.heading: The 10-Item Packing System for Light Travelers
placeholderSections.body: For summer trips of 5–7 days, this 10-item system fills a personal item bag and avoids all baggage fees entirely. (1) Washable travel pants — one pair of lightweight hiking pants or dark jeans, worn on the plane. (2) Quick-dry shorts — one pair, doubles as swim trunks or beach bottoms. (3) Neutral t-shirt — two, preferably merino wool (sweat-resistant, wear 2–3 times between washes) or quick-dry polyester. (4) Button-down shirt or blouse — one that works for dinner or nicer outings. (5) Underwear — three pairs (quick-dry, wash in sink). (6) Socks — two pairs (for hiking or everyday). (7) Swimwear — one set, packed in a small dry bag. (8) Versatile shoes — wear one pair of comfortable sneakers or walking shoes that work for both hiking and casual dining. (9) Toiletries kit — travel-sized shampoo, sunscreen, toothpaste, deodorant, solid soap (no liquids over 3.4oz for carry-on). (10) Light outer layer — a packable windbreaker or hoodie for evenings and air-conditioned planes. This system fits into an 18x14x8-inch personal item bag (free on every airline) and covers a week of summer travel. The trick: do a sink wash of underwear and t-shirts halfway through your trip. Dry time on summer evenings is 2–3 hours.
